coredata.dc:title: Efficient decontamination and detoxification of phenols by photocatalytic CQDs@Ag3PO4: Re-insights into the targeted reduction and coupling processes of ·O2 – and 1O2

coredata.dc:description: Understanding of 1O2 and ·O2 – is still mostly restricted to the oxidation properties for open-ring degradation of pollutants. Here we treated the P-nitrophenol (PNP) with toxic and antioxidant nitro group as the targeted pollutant with the prepared carbon quantum dots-modified silver phosphate (CQDs@Ag3PO4) photocatalyst. Quenching experiments, electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy characterizations, probe experiments, and comparative tests on TOC removal have confirmed photoinduced holes (h+), singlet oxygen (1O2) and superoxide anion (·O2 –) induced by photogenerated electrons and molecular oxygen are the primary reactive species involved in the aerobic system. Through targeted experiments in the Xanthine/XOD and NaClO/H2O2 systems HPLC-MS and Fukui Function, it reveals the targeted reduction of ·O2 – and coupling role of 1O2 and their synergistic effect on decontamination and detoxification of PNP. Moreover, the replacement of ring-opening degradation with oligomerization by 1O2 induced follows a new carbon transfer pathway. Accordingly, four PNP elimination pathways were proposed, and the key intermediates had low toxicity predicted by T.E.S.T. software, including the oligomers via coupling reaction. Conclusively, this work provides a comprehensive view of 1O2 and ·O2 – and proposes an efficient strategy for realizing pollutant control by manipulating reactive species with solar energy utilization.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Understanding of 1O2 and ·O2 – is still mostly restricted to the oxidation properties for open-ring degradation of pollutants. Here we treated the P-nitrophenol (PNP) with toxic and antioxidant nitro group as the targeted pollutant with the prepared carbon quantum dots-modified silver phosphate (CQDs@Ag3PO4) photocatalyst. Quenching experiments, electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy characterizations, probe experiments, and comparative tests on TOC removal have confirmed photoinduced holes (h+), singlet oxygen (1O2) and superoxide anion (·O2 –) induced by photogenerated electrons and molecular oxygen are the primary reactive species involved in the aerobic system. Through targeted experiments in the Xanthine/XOD and NaClO/H2O2 systems HPLC-MS and Fukui Function, it reveals the targeted reduction of ·O2 – and coupling role of 1O2 and their synergistic effect on decontamination and detoxification of PNP. Moreover, the replacement of ring-opening degradation with oligomerization by 1O2 induced follows a new carbon transfer pathway. Accordingly, four PNP elimination pathways were proposed, and the key intermediates had low toxicity predicted by T.E.S.T. software, including the oligomers via coupling reaction. Conclusively, this work provides a comprehensive view of 1O2 and ·O2 – and proposes an efficient strategy for realizing pollutant control by manipulating reactive species with solar energy utilization.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: P-nitrophenol (PNP) and its derivatives are typical nitroaromatic compounds that are employed extensively in a variety of industrial applications, including the production of dyes, pesticides, plasticizers, wood preservatives, and rubber chemicals [1]. They are toxic and refractory pollutants in natural water and industrial wastewater, and the majority of them are considered priority toxic pollutants by the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)[2]. However, the detoxification of wastewaters containing nitroaromatic compounds is challenging due to their resistance to chemical or biological oxidation, which is attributed to the electro-withdrawing effect of the nitro group[3]. Consequently, identifying an environmentally friendly reduction method may be one of the most promising avenues for the detoxification of nitroaromatic compounds and improving its biodegradability[3].

Photocatalytic solar-energy conversion is being drawn increasing attention due to its robust new methods for water purification and environmental protection at lower cost and energy consumption[4,5]. The photocatalytic effect is exerted by the redox reaction caused by the photoinduced electrons (e-) and holes (h+) generated on the heterogeneous solid surface of the photocatalyst[6]. The reactive oxygen species (ROS) are generated from the conversion of molecular oxygen through charge carrier transfer and exciton transfer[7,8]. In the charge carrier pathway, the excitation of photoelectrons and holes in a localized manner generates free radicals, such as superoxide anion (·O2 –) and hydroxyl radicals (·OH). In contrast, the exciton pathway results in the production of singlet oxygen (1O2) through the transfer of energy to O₂ [9–11]. They are green, cheap, and readily available, leading to their wide use in the fields of environmental chemistry and biochemistry[12–14]. ROS are important intermediates in the photocatalytic process. Consequently, an investigation into the generation, interconversion, and redox processes of ROS is of significant theoretical value in the context of understanding the photocatalytic mechanism and the selective generation of ROS with enhanced photocatalytic ability for the degradation of refractory pollutants.

Much research has been conducted to develop and enhance efficient photocatalytic materials to enhance photocatalytic efficiency. In contrast, there is still a lack of comprehensive understanding of the reactive oxygen species involved in these processes, which may significantly impact the photocatalytic degradation pathways of pollutants. Among ROS, ·O2 – was widely used in the field of pollutant treatment while almost focused on selective oxidation properties[9,15]. It has been reported that ·O2 – can effectively degrade chlorinated hydrocarbons and polyhalogenated aromatic compounds, such as decomposing hexachlorobenzene or polychlorinated biphenyls into bicarbonate and halide ions[16–18]. It also can realize efficient photocatalytic oxidation of phenolic compounds, such as phenol[19], pentachlorophenol[20], and 2,4-DCP[21]. However, ·O2 – cannot directly react with acetaminophen (ACT)[22]. Do we appropriately understand the role of ·O2 – in the degradation of pollutants? Interestingly, ·O2 – has a reduction potential [E0 (O2/·O2 –)] of −0.33 VNHE and an oxidation potential [E0 (1O2/·O2 –)] of + 0.81 VNHE [23]. Accordingly, targeted reduction in electro-withdrawing groups of highly oxidized organic compounds is overlooked as the trigger of its selectivity.

1O2 is one of the most commonly proposed non-radical species for the preferential destruction of organic pollutants in AOPs, considered to be a promising alternative to non-selective •OH[24,25]. The highly reactive 1O2 exhibits a mild oxidation ability (2.2 VNHE), which is weaker than that of ·OH (2.7 VNHE). Besides, 1O2 can directly oxidize unsaturated organics through electron transfer or electrophilic additions (e.g., 1,2-,1,3-, or 1,4-cycloaddition dependent on substrates) due to the electrophilic nature[26]. It is appliable for preferential oxidation of high-priority pollutants in many fields and is widely used for the remediation of polluted water[27,28]. Similar to ·O2 –, much of the research on 1O2 has been on its oxidation properties, such as participating in the degradation of diclofenac[29], 2,4-DCP[30], and BPA[31]. Though the coupling reaction induced by 1O2 has been reported in organic synthesis chemistry[32], this process should have received wider attention in the treatment of organic pollutants. Contrary to the mineralization of organics, the replacement of pollutant degradation with the 1O2-induced coupling reaction facilitates the transformation of the pollutant into macromolecular humus, thereby reducing carbon emissions.

As one of the environment-friendly sustainable technologies, silver phosphate (Ag3PO4) with a narrow band gap has attracted considerable attention since its discovery by Yi et al. in 2010[33,34]. The valence band potential of Ag3PO4 is more negative than the H2O/·OH potential (2.72 eV) and more positive than the O2/·O2 – potential (−0.33 eV), which makes it difficult to generate ·OH and ·O2 –, which tends to contributes a lot in selective degradation of pollutants. Due to the limitation, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were introduced and the CQDs@Ag3PO4-based studies have been widely conducted to improve photocatalysis stability performance. For example, Zhuo Chen et.al.[34] developed a three-dimensional network space Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH to realize efficient and stable degradation of TC; Ning Shao et.al.[35] synthesized a novel 3D core–shell CQDs/Ag3PO4@benzoxazine tetrapods to photodegrade sulfamethoxazole; Yifei Wang et.al.[36] fabricated a Z-scheme Y-Ag3PO4/CQDs/BiVO4 photocatalyst for atenolol degradation. However, previous studies focused on improving the performance of the photocatalysts and better degradation efficiency for organic pollutants, with minimal discussion of the mechanisms by which the active species are generated and transformed within them. Moreover, in the systems of CQDs@Ag3PO4-based photocatalyst having been reported, the active species are predominantly h+, ·OH, and ·O2 –, whlie only reported the selective oxidation properties of ·O2 –. Furthermore, there is little report on the contribution of 1O2 in this system, especially its coupling process. Additionally, the toxicity of oligomers via coupling reaction remains unclear, which is important to the new carbon transfer pathway via oligomerization but not ring-opening degradation.

Therefore, to provide a comprehensive view of 1O2 and ·O2 –, we purposely treated p-nitrophenol (PNP) as the targeted pollutant with the CQDs@ Ag3PO4 photocatalyst aimed to highlight the targeted reduction of ·O2 – and coupling role of 1O2, and their synergistic effect on decontamination and detoxification for refractory pollutants containing electron withdrawing groups. In conclusion, the objectives of this study are as follows: (I) to evaluate the physicochemical properties and photocatalytic performance of the photocatalyst; (II) to determine the formation, transformation and their contributions of ROS in the system; (III) to reveal the targeted reduction and coupling reaction pathway of PNP under the dominant photogenerated carrier and ROS; (IV) to evaluate the toxicity of intermediates and products.
A detailed list of chemicals is offered in Text S1 and the process of preparation. The preparation method of pure Ag3PO4 is provided in Text S2.

As illustrated in Fig. 1
a, the CQDs@Ag3PO4 composite photocatalyst was synthesized via the co-precipitation method. Initially, 0.3044 g AgNO3 was added to 30 mL of ultrapure water, which was then stirred for 30 min. Subsequently, 0.2140 g Na2HPO4·12H2O (10 mL) and a CQDs solution of varying concentrations (0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, and 3.0 mL) were added to the reactor. The beaker containing the Na2HPO4·12H2O solution was then transferred and cleaned with 10 mL of ultrapure water. The solution was stirred for 12 h, filtered and washed with ultrapure water three to five times, and then dried at 60℃. The isabelline solids were collected and thoroughly ground. Finally, the CQDs@Ag3PO4 composite photocatalyst was obtained and designated as 0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 2.5, and 3.0 mL CQDs@Ag3PO4, respectively, based on the different dosages of CQDs.

The dosage of composite materials (CQDs@Ag3PO4) in the photocatalytic system was 0.15 g/L, and the initial concentration of target pollutants was both 10 μM. Firstly, the solution pH was adjusted to 6.0 and stirred magnetically in the dark for 30 min to reach the adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Then, the reaction system was put in the visible light reaction instrument, and 0.5 mL samples were taken at a predetermined time, filtered by a 0.22 μm aqueous phase needle filter, and injected into a 1.5 mL chromatography bottle. Finally, the concentration of PNP at different reaction time points was determined by high-performance liquid chromatography. All experiments were conducted in duplicates or more. The photochemical reaction devices used in the experiment consist of a photochemical reaction chamber (CEL-LB70), a photolysis device (CEL-LAB500), a light source generator, a long arc xenon lamp, a filter, and a circulating cooling device, as Figure S1 shows. All equipment was purchased from Beijing China Education Au-light Co., Ltd. The light source was a 500 W-long arc xenon lamp equipped with a detachable filter that can cut and filtrate light below 420 nm. Therefore, the wavelength range of the light provided in the reactor is 420–1100 nm. The working voltage and current are 50 V and 6–10 A, respectively. Besides, the working distance between the reactor and the long arc xenon lamp is 75 nm.

All pollutants used in this paper were measured using high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, Agilent 1260) equipped with a UV detector and an Agilent C18 column (4.5 × 250 mm, 5 µm). For PNP and PAP detection, a methanol–water mixture (60:40, v/v) flowing at the constant rate of 1.0 mL/min and a methanol-hydrogen phosphate mixture of 8 mM KH2PO4 and 4 mM NaH2PO4 (15:85, v/v) flowing at the constant rate of 0.8 mL/min respectively, and a column temperature of 30℃ and 35℃ respectively, and detection wavelength of 317 and 240 nm respectively were used. The detailed characterization methods of prepared photocatalysts are provided in Text S3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Successful cooperation of CQDs and Ag3PO4
ce:section.ce:para: Notably, a significant color darkening of the prepared 0.5 mL CQDs@ Ag3PO4 composite was observed compared to Ag3PO4 (AP), as shown in Fig. 1 b and 1d. To investigate this difference, we conducted scanning electron microscopy (SEM) observations on both Ag3PO4 and the 0.5 mL CQDs@AP composite. Fig. 1 b-e shows that the surface of the composite material has numerous short protrusions and transforms into finer particles in contrast with the Ag3PO4 with a smooth surface. Additionally, the nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms and pore size distribution results ( Figure S2 ) confirm that the composite material has a larger specific surface area and pore size compared to Ag3PO4.

The apparent darker color seems to be attributed to the finer particles, but the exact reason for the change in morphology is unclear. It is suspected that the introduction of CQDs may be related. To support this hypothesis, the Energy Dispersive X-Ray Spectroscopy (EDX) was conducted on 0.5 mL CQDs@Ag3PO4, which revealed the presence of 14.67 % C element (Fig. 2
a). The uniform distribution of C, O, P, and Ag elements was further confirmed by SEM elemental mapping (Fig. 1 f-i) and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) full-scan spectra (Fig. 2 d). The presence of the C element initially verifies the successful loading of CQDs.

Further, we compared the X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) patterns ( Fig. 2 b) of the prepared CQDs@AP composites with Ag3PO4, and it can be seen that although no new diffraction peaks appear, the intensity of Ag3PO4 decreases with the increase of CQDs content. The Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrogram of the composites ( Fig. 2 c) reveals the characteristic peaks of Ag3PO4 and the characteristic peaks corresponding to the carbon g-bands. The XPS spectra detects the characteristic peaks of Ag 3d5/2, Ag 3d3/2, P5+, and Ag-O (Fig. 2 e-g), which provides evidence for the existence of Ag3PO4. Furthermore, three separate peaks were detected at C1s ( Fig. 2 h), corresponding to the CQDs [37], the indeterminate hydrocarbons [38], and the impurity structure of carbon[39]. This directly proves that CQDs were successfully introduced into Ag3PO4. Therefore, in response to the previous hypothesis, it is the loading of CQDs that leads to the transformation of AP into finer particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Affecting factors of the CQDs@AP-based photocatalytic property
ce:section.ce:para:
The previous discussion has shown that the introduction of CQDs impacts the morphology of the material. However, it remains to be seen whether this will result in any unexpected changes in the photocatalytic performance. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy was carried out for 0.5 mL CQDs@AP and AP. Fig. 3
a shows the absorption edge of AP is approximately 534 nm, while that of 0.5 mL CQDs@AP composite is at 565 nm, indicating a stronger visible light absorption capacity. The Kubelka Munk curves ( Fig. 3 b) also indicate that the loading of CQDs can reduce the Eg value of the composite. The calculated values of Eg, EVB, and ECB for the photocatalysts are combined in Table S1 . Text S4 provides a detailed explanation of the calculation method. The redshift of the light absorption edge and the narrowing of the bandgap width tend to indicate superior photoactivity and photostability, which is the basis of the CQDs@AP with better photocatalytic performance.

The photocatalytic performance of composite materials with different gradients was measured with PNP degradation as the evaluation criterion. The experimental results are shown in Fig. 4
a. It can be seen that the addition of high doses of CQDs affects the adsorption characteristics of the catalyst. CQDs exhibit a dose effect on photocatalytic activity. The degradation efficiency peaks at 0.5 mL of CQDs, reaching 100 %. Therefore, the optimal preparation ratio should be 0.5 mL of CQDs and 0.25 g of Ag3PO4. This composite will be used for subsequent experiments to further analyze the underlying mechanism of the reaction system.

The initial pH of the solution has an impact on the form of the catalyst, its surface charge conditions, and the structural properties of the reaction substrate. Figure S3 shows that the nitro group in p-nitrophenol maintains its electron-withdrawing properties over a wide pH range. However, when the pH exceeds 7.3, the phenolic hydroxyl group ionizes with a negative charge, which affects the electron absorption properties of the nitro group. As seen in Fig. 4 c, the adsorption performance for PNP decreases with increasing pH. This suggests that the ionization of the phenolic hydroxyl group weakens the electron-withdrawing ability of the nitro group. At pH of 3.0, the adsorption of PNP reaches about 27 %, but its removal rate during the reaction stage is less than 8 %. It can be speculated that the composite catalyst loses its photocatalytic ability in a strongly acidic environment due to the dissolution of silver phosphate. Within the initial pH range of 5.0 to 9.0, there is no significant difference in the degradation performance of 0.5 mL CQDs@AP towards PNP, indicating that the catalyst has a broad application prospect and also suggesting that the main active species in the system have strong adaptability to pH.

The effect of different coexisting ions on the reaction system is shown in Fig. 4 d. The results indicate that the presence of SO4 2- and NO3 – does not affect the degradation performance of PNP, while the presence of CO3 2– and Cl- weakens the catalytic activity of the system. Compared to the control group, the k values for the first 60 min decreased by 92.3 % and 76.8 %, respectively. The former is mainly attributed to the competition of CO3 2– for some of the photogenerated holes (ORP of approximately 2.9 V), which are converted into carbonate radicals with a lower redox potential (ORP of approximately 1.78 V)[40,41]. The latter is due to the competition of Cl- for Ag+, which triggers changes in crystal type and structure.

To investigate the stability of the 0.5 mL CQDs@AP, we conducted cycling experiments comparing it with Ag3PO4. As shown in Fig. 4 b, the photocatalytic performance of both materials decreased over time during the cycling process. However, the 0.5 mL CQDs@AP exhibited superior degradation performance in the subsequent cycles. The improved stability of the composite catalyst is speculated to be attributed to the transfer of some photogenerated electrons by CQDs, which possess both electron acceptor and electron donor properties. This transfer is likely to mitigate the photocorrosion of silver phosphate and extend the lifespan of the 0.5 mL CQDs@AP.

To validate the inference above, it is necessary to analyze the migration direction of photogenerated electrons in 0.5 mL CQDs@AP. A photochemical deposition experiment was conducted using H2PtCl6·6H2O as the precursor and water as the hole scavenger. The TEM image in Fig. S4 a clearly shows the deposition of Pt metal particles on the CQDs. The HRTEM image (Fig. S4 a) shows lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.226 nm, which are in line with the (111) crystal plane of face-centered cubic Pt. The TEM-EDS spectrum in Fig. S4 b further confirms the presence of Pt. A combination of the principle of photodeposition (Text S5) and the decrease in the valence state of the metal ion precursor indicates that a photoreduction reaction has occurred near the CQDs. Nevertheless, it is crucial to highlight that using CQDs alone is insufficient in generating sufficient photogenerated electrons to effectively reduce platinum ions into metallic platinum. Therefore, the photogenerated electrons present on CQDs originate from the closely contacting Ag3PO4. In other words, the photogenerated electrons produced by Ag3PO4 are transferred to CQDs, thereby facilitating the photoreduction process of metal ions.

The results demonstrate that the introduction of CQDs serves as both an electron acceptor and donor, facilitating the transfer of electrons generated by the photoexcitation of Ag3PO4. This promotes the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, mitigates photocorrosion, and enhances the catalyst's stability. In conclusion, the introduction of CQDs contributes to enhancing the photostability with a wider range of pH applications.

Mineralization is the most direct pathway for pollutant degradation. We determined the concentration of PNP and the value of TOC during the reaction under aerobic and aerobic conditions, as shown in Fig. 4 e-4f. Interestingly, the removal of PNP reached 100 % in both conditions, but the reduction of TOC was not as high, with the anaerobic condition being better than the aerobic one, but only 28.21 %. Photogenerated holes have strong oxidation ability, which can directly oxidize organic pollutants or react with water to generate ·OH[42]. The removal of TOC is primarily attributed to the action of h+, yet the remaining over 70 % of the degradation effect remains unclear. Therefore, it is crucial to further investigate the active oxygen species generated and transformed during the reaction and their contribution to PNP degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photogenerated carrier separation and generation of active oxygen species
ce:section.ce:para:
The main active species for 0.5 mL CQDs@AP photocatalyst degradation of PNP were detected by capture experiments, which helps derive the charge transfer path in this composite[43,44]. Methanol (MeOH), tert-butanol (TBA), p-benzoquinone (BQ), and furfuryl alcohol (FFA) were used as quenching agents for h+, ·OH, ·O2 – and 1O2, respectively, with TBA and BQ dosage of 100 mM and 0.2 mM respectively, and the rest of 10 mM. The estimation of the second-order rate constant of each quencher with ROS and the selection of quencher dosage were further illustrated in Text S6. The degradation trend of PNP after the addition of quenching agents was shown in Fig. 5
a. The PNP degradation was almost completely inhibited by the excess FFA at 1000 times of the organic matter concentration, and the removal effect of the system with the addition of BQ and MeOH, including the adsorption of the composite catalyst, was significantly inhibited, with the removal rates of 24.25 % and 67.62 %, respectively. In contrast, the addition of 10,000-fold concentration of TBA had a negligible effect on the reaction system, indicating that h+, ·O2 – and 1O2 were produced in the system, and all three were contributing to PNP degradation, with 1O2 playing the dominant role, followed by ·O2 –, and h+ being slightly weaker.

The electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) technique was carried out on the 0.5 mL CQDs@AP composite to further determine the types of active species generated in the photocatalytic process. 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-n-oxide (DMPO), 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine oxide (TEMPO) were used as spin trapping agents to capture the generated instantaneous free radicals. According to Fig. 5 b-d, it can be seen that there were clear signals of DMPO-·O2 – and TEMP-1O2 in the identification results, and with the prolongation of the light time, the signals of TEMPO were weakening, while the signals of DMPO-·O2 – and TEMP-1O2 were strengthening[45], indicating that h+, ·O2 – and 1O2 were produced in the 0.5 mL CQDs@AP system.

The results of free radical quenching and EPR detection experiments confirm that the active species generated in the 0.5 mL CQDs@AP system are 1O2, ·O2 –, and h+. While the generation processes of the latter two have been widely accepted, the process of 1O2 remains unclear. To investigate the generation and transformation of singlet oxygen, we utilized the targeted reaction between 9,10-diphenylanthracene (DPA) and 1O2 to produce a specific stable endoperoxide (DPAO2)[46] in the 0.5 mL CQDs@AP system. Fig. 6
a shows a molecular weight of 363.1391 in the PNP solution. Secondary mass spectrometry analysis revealed molecular weights of 105, 121, 257, and 285, consistent with the cleavage and transformation process of the peroxide within DPA ( Fig. 6 b). This finding confirms the generation of DPAO2, revealing the production of 1O2 in the 0.5 mL CQDs@AP system. However, the system with additional BQ did not detect any DPAO2 molecular weight, and the presence of a molecular weight of 330.1407 indicates that DPA was not consumed. Therefore, it can be concluded that 1O2 is converted from ·O2 –.

As a result, a detailed explanation of active species production in 0.5 mL CQDs@AP system is described as Fig. 7 shows. Under visible light irradiation, the electrons on the VB of Ag3PO4 can easily be excited and jump to the CB, and photogenerated holes can be formed on the VB. The CQDs with excellent electron transport properties can act as a photogenerated electron transport layer. The photogenerated electrons on the CB of Ag3PO4 could transfer into the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) level of CQDs as electron reservoirs for charge-carriers separation in the Ag3PO4 [34,47,48]. Consequently, the more negative CB of CQDs could generate more ·O2 –. Therefore, CQDs can weaken the photocorrosion phenomenon of Ag3PO4 and enhance the stability of CQDs@AP, as well as promote the production of the active substance ·O2 – with O2/·O2 – potential (−0.33 VNHE)[23]. Then, the photogenerated h+ remains in the VB of Ag3PO4 and has a stronger oxidation performance, which can further oxidize ·O2 – to 1O2 with an oxidation potential of 0.81 VNHE. Accordingly, the rapid migration of photogenerated electrons on CQDs can effectively reduce the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, and further enhance the catalytic performance of the composites. However, the photogenerated holes cannot oxidize the water molecules to produce ·OH because the VB of Ag3PO4 was lower than the H2O/·OH potential (2.72 VNHE). Overall, the active species in the 0.5 mL CQDs@AP photocatalytic reaction system were 1O2, ·O2 – and h+, involving the following equations[49]:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed decontamination and detoxification pathways of PNP
ce:section.ce:para:
To further reveal the elemination pathways of PNP in the 0.5 mL CQDs@AP system, the reactivity of the different active species toward PNP needs to be investigated separately. The classic enzyme catalysis of Xanthine and Xanthine oxidase (XOD) can specifically produce ·O2 – [50], so the target pollutant was put into the Xanthine/XOD reaction system to monitor the change of concentration. As shown in Fig. 8
a, PNP was removed by about 10.58 % in the Xanthine/XOD reaction system within 2 h, which indicated that ·O2 – can directly act on PNP.

To further illustrate the accuracy of the PNP removal rate of 10.58 % in the Xanthine/XOD reaction system rather than an experimental error, high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) analysis was performed on the reaction endpoint solution. The results are shown in Fig. 8 b. The molecular weights of 108.0223, 116.9293, and 138.0214 can be clearly retrieved from the primary mass spectra, indicating the production of p-aminophenol (PAP) and small molecular acids in the system. In other words, the selective reduction and oxidative degradation of PNP were achieved.

Furthermore, during the HPLC-MS analysis of the PNP intermediates, the presence of PAP in the 0.5 mL CQDs@AP photocatalysis system was detected. The complete structural formulas of the obtained intermediates are presented in Table S2 . Therefore, the formation of PAP was observed in both the Xanthine/XOD system with a single active species and in the 0.5 mL CQDs@AP system with multiple active species. Figure S5 shows that both PAP and aniline compound concentrations increased during the 120 min, indicating a continuous reduction of PNP to PAP and other amine compounds by ·O2 –. PAP is the main reduction product, suggesting that ·O2 – has a high reduction capacity, demonstrating its dual nature of reduction and oxidation.

Based on the above analysis, it was known that there was no ·OH generation in the 0.5 mL CQDs@AP reaction system, and the photogenerated holes were supposed to be involved in the process of 1O2 generation. Does this affect its direct oxidative degradation of organic pollutants? To clarify this question, nitrogen was continuously passed into the reaction system to weaken the competition and consumption of h+ by ·O2 –, and the degradation and mineralization of PNP were measured. As shown in Fig. 4 e-4f, the degradation activity and mineralization degree of the system for PNP had been improved in the nitrogen atmosphere, which indicated that h+ had stronger degradation and mineralization ability for the target pollutant than 1O2. Therefore, if we want to improve the mineralization ability of the 0.5 mL CQDs@AP system, the directed guidance of h+ generation is an effective means. Meanwhile, the above result also proved that 1O2 was produced by the h+ oxidation of ·O2 –.

To further demonstrate the role of 1O2 in the PNP system, we injected DPA into the 0.5 mL CQDs@AP system both in the presence and absence of PNP, followed by HPLC-MS analysis. The results revealed that the primary and secondary signals of DPAO2 in the aqueous solution were more than twice as intense as those in the PNP solution (Fig. 8 c), indicating that 1O2 can react directly with PNP. Therefore, all active species, including 1O2, ·O2 –, and h+, can directly attack PNP. The degradation and removal of PNP are likely due to the oxidation of 1O2 and the reduction–oxidation of ·O2 –. Mineralization is attributed to the oxidation of h+.

To further reveal the reaction pathway of PNP in the 0.5 mL CQDs@AP system, we first applied the B3LYP-D3BJ/TZVP theory to fully optimize the PNP structure, making its structure characterized by the local energy minimum of the potential energy surface. Then Multiwfn software was utilized to calculate the Fukui Function, which was proposed to predict the reactive sites where reactants and their intermediates may flourish[51].

The detailed data for the Fukui function are presented in Table S3 . The f value represents the reactivity of atoms at corresponding positions, with a larger f- value indicating a higher tendency towards electrophilic reactions, a larger f+ value indicating a higher tendency towards nucleophilic reactions, and a larger f0 value indicating a higher susceptibility to attack by radicals. Based on these data, we used a visual molecular dynamics program to plot isosurface maps of the Fukui function, as Figure S6 shows, where the larger green areas indicate larger values of the Fukui function for the corresponding atoms. By combining the identified intermediate products with the results of the Fukui function and relevant literature, we inferred the possible degradation pathway of PNP in the 0.5 mL CQDs@AP reaction system, as shown in Fig. 9 .

In pathway I and II, PNP was detached from the nitro or hydroxyl group by catalytic oxidation, followed by mineralization under the strong oxidizing action of holes, ultimately converting into carbon dioxide and water.

In pathway III, due to the reduction of ·O2 – and high f+ value of 11 N, it tends to attack the position of 11 N in the nitro group, targeting the reduction of PNP to form PAP (P3, m/z = 109)[52]. PAP subsequently undergoes further attack and oxidation by ·O2 –, leading to the formation of hydroquinone (P4, m/z = 110) and p-benzoquinone (P5, m/z = 108). Finally, these intermediates undergo ring opening to form small molecular acids (P6, m/z = 118). This pathway aligns with the reaction pathway of PNP in the Xanthine/XOD system, further confirming the dual redox of ·O2 – in this system.

In Pathway IV, as shown in Table S3 , positions 1C and 5C have high f-values, indicating susceptibility to attack by 1O2. As a result, PNP can undergo coupling reactions at these positions to form dimers (P7, m/z = 276)[53]. To confirm this process, we used the H2O2/NaClO system to generate 1O2 and react with PNP. The resulting reaction solution was analyzed by LC-MS. The results are shown in Fig. 8 d. The primary mass spectrometry detected a dimer with a molecular weight of 277.0592, indicating its formation in the system. Figure S5 shows the detection of other amine compounds in the 0.5 mL CQDs@AP system. The intermediates were analyzed using LC-MS ( Figure S7 ), and it can be inferred that the dimer undergoes similar reactions as described in Pathway II. The reduction of the nitro group to an amino group by ·O2 – results in the formation of P8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The decrease in toxicity of intermediates
ce:section.ce:para:
Toxicity evaluation is an essential strategy for assessing ecological risks. Therefore, the Toxicity Estimation Software Tool (T.E.S.T.) based on the quantitative structure–activity relationship (QSAR) was used to predict the toxicity of parent compounds and intermediates (Fig. 10
and
Table S4 ). The biological toxicity of PNP and its decomposition intermediates was evaluated by measuring the Oral rat Ld50 (Fig. 10 a). The results showed that the toxicity of the intermediates was reduced during the degradation, even to “low-toxic”, especially for stable intermediates of P6 and P8. Although the intermediates toxicant for Developmental toxicity increased, most of them are non-toxicity (Fig. 10 b). Additionally, the severity of environmental impact is directly proportional to the magnitude of the bioaccumulation factor. The bioaccumulation factor (BAF) of PNP is 5.70, but some intermediates could be increased, such as P3, P4, P7, and P8, yet others’ bioaccumulation factor is decreased (Fig. 10 c). For mutagenesis, only P8 exhibited a positive mutagenesis result, while the other intermediates were negative (Fig. 10 d). The toxicity analysis further indicated that ·O2 – had an advanced reduction-detoxification effect and the key intermediates of PNP in the 0.5 mL CQDs@AP system is environmentally friendly.

The cooperation of CQDs and Ag3PO4 improves the photocatalytic stability of the catalyst with broadened visible light absorption range (λ ≤ 565 nm) and narrowed band gap width (Eg = 2.25 eV) of composite and reduced conduction band potancial (<-0.33 eV). Inhibition of the TOC removal due to the oxygen presence revealed that their transformation mechanism of 1O2, ·O2 –, and h+. 1O2 plays the dominant role in the system. The degradation pathway analysis based on HPLC-MS and Fuki function proves the generation process of eight intermediates, of which toxicity evaluation by T.E.S.T. software illustrates the decreasing toxicity, which is attributed to the targeted reduction and coupling effects of ·O2 – and 1O2 and their synergistic effect. This study provides a comprehensive view of 1O2 and ·O2 –, follows a new carbon transfer pathway via oligomerization but not ring-opening degradation, and proposes a strategy to realize pollutant decontamination and detoxification with solar energy by manipulating the reactive species in a controllable reaction pathway.
